URLs with secrets are routinely captured by proxies, TLS-terminating middleboxes, crash/error reporters, and any code that logs request URLs. While the current catch logs only e.message, embedding a credential in the URL is a standing leakage risk and makes accidental exposure (future logging, redirects) easy.
failure_scenario: A future log line, HTTP-debug env var, or intermediary captures the outbound URL; the Gemini key is exposed and can be used to bill/abuse the account.
fix_suggestion: Send the key via the `x-goog-api-key` request header instead of the `key` query parameter, keeping it out of URLs and logs.
reasoning: Line 72 of src/gateway/ollama.ts builds the request URL as `https://generativelanguage.googleapis.com/v1beta/models/${config.gemini.model}:generateContent?key=${config.gemini.apiKey}` and passes it to fetch() on line 73. The secret is embedded in the URL query string exactly as the finding states. This is a recognized credential-hygiene weakness: URLs (unlike request bodies/headers) are commonly captured by forward/reverse proxies, TLS-terminating middleboxes, access logs, browser/HTTP-client debug traces, and error reporters. The Gemini API explicitly supports the `x-goog-api-key` header as the safer alternative, so the fix is trivial and non-breaking. The severity is correctly rated low: this is a standing leakage/hardening risk, not an actively exploitable path. In the current code the only logging is `console.warn("[gemini] fallback failed:", (e as Error).message)` on line 93, which logs only the error message and not the URL, so there is no active leak today — the risk is latent (future logging, redirects, intermediaries), which matches a "low" rating rather than medium/high. Note: the finding's dimension label "command-injection-gateways" is a misnomer (there is no command injection here), but the security concern described in the title/body is accurate.

title: API key compared with non-constant-time string equality
description: The auth check uses `auth === \`Bearer ${API_KEY}\`` (line 169), a short-circuiting string comparison whose timing varies with the length of the matching prefix. When the server is exposed over a tunnel, this is a remotely observable timing side-channel for recovering the key byte-by-byte.
failure_scenario: An attacker with network access to the tunneled endpoint sends many requests with guessed Authorization values and measures response latency to progressively recover AURA_API_KEY, then gains full authenticated control of all OS-action endpoints.
fix_suggestion: Compare using a constant-time function: decode both sides to Buffers of equal length and use `crypto.timingSafeEqual`, returning 401 uniformly on any mismatch or length difference.
reasoning: The cited code is present verbatim in src/server.ts. The auth middleware (registered only when AURA_API_KEY is set) does `const auth = req.headers.authorization;` on line 168 and `if (auth === \`Bearer ${API_KEY}\`) return next();` on line 169, then falls through to a 401. JavaScript's `===` on strings is not constant-time — V8 first compares lengths, then compares byte-by-byte and short-circuits on the first mismatch, so the comparison duration correlates with the length of the shared prefix. This is exactly a non-constant-time secret comparison, so the finding is factually correct. The fix suggestion (crypto.timingSafeEqual on equal-length Buffers, uniform 401 on mismatch or length difference) is the right remedy.\n\nSeverity, however, is overstated at medium. In practice this is a poor exploitation target: (1) the per-character timing delta from V8 string comparison is on the order of nanoseconds, dwarfed by network jitter across a tunnel — recovering a key byte-by-byte remotely would require an impractical number of requests and statistical averaging; (2) the endpoint is behind two rate limiters (30/min on /api/say, 60/min on /api/hrv, and a global 100/min per line 140) which severely throttles the request volume any timing attack needs; (3) auth is off by default and only enabled when the operator sets AURA_API_KEY. It is a legitimate best-practice violation worth fixing, but the realistic remote-key-recovery scenario is marginal, so low is the accurate rating.

title: tau = c_fa/(c_fa+c_fn) yields NaN when both calibrated costs are zero
description: tau is computed as c_fa / (c_fa + c_fn) with no guard for a zero denominator (gate.ts:122). c_fa/c_fn originate from soul.cost_weights (soul.ts) which a misconfigured or hand-edited SOUL.md could set to 0/0 for a context. calibrateCosts passes such statics straight through when bootstrapping. A NaN tau makes both `utility > tau + MARGIN` and `utility < tau - MARGIN` false, silently forcing every borderline decision down the slow path and emitting NaN into the audit record (tau.toFixed prints 'NaN').
failure_scenario: SOUL.md defines cost_weights.quiet_hours: { false_alarm: 0, missed_help: 0 }. During quiet hours, tau = 0/0 = NaN; the audit log records tau 'NaN' and the fast-accept/fast-reject branches never trigger, degrading gate behavior in a way that is hard to diagnose.
fix_suggestion: Clamp/validate cost weights at load time (reject non-positive values, or floor at a small epsilon) and guard tau: `const denom = c_fa + c_fn; const tau = denom > 0 ? c_fa/denom : 0.5;`.
reasoning: gate.ts:122 is exactly `const tau = c_fa / (c_fa + c_fn);` with no denominator guard. c_fa/c_fn come from cal.c_fa/cal.c_fn (lines 116-117), which originate from soul.cost_weights[context_label].false_alarm/.missed_help. In bootstrapping mode calibration.ts:85-92 returns the statics verbatim; in calibrated mode calibration.ts:107-108 multiplies statics by scale factors in [1,2], so a static 0 stays 0 in both modes. loadSoul() in soul.ts:41-72 does NO validation of cost weight values — it parses the YAML block directly (soul.ts:43-52) with no non-negativity/epsilon check. Therefore a SOUL.md (or the parsed fallback path) with both false_alarm:0 and missed_help:0 for a context produces tau = 0/0 = NaN. NaN then makes `utility > tau+MARGIN` (line 147) and `utility < tau-MARGIN` (line 164) both false, forcing the slow path (line 182+), where regret_if_silent = p_need*0 = 0 and cost_if_speak = (1-p_accept)*0 = 0, so intervene = 0 > 0 = false. The GateDecision.tau field is NaN and is returned/recorded. The shipped SOUL.md (lines 28-43) never sets both to zero and the code fallback default (soul.ts:46-52) is also safe, so the trigger requires a deliberate or erroneous config edit — matching the finder's own framing (misconfigured/hand-edited SOUL.md). Impact is degraded gate behavior + NaN in the audit record, not a crash. Low severity is accurate.
repro: Edit SOUL.md cost_weights: quiet_hours: { false_alarm: 0.0, missed_help: 0.0 }. During quiet hours classifyContext returns "quiet_hours" (gate.ts:57-58); staticWeights = {false_alarm:0, missed_help:0}; with <5 samples calibrateCosts returns c_fa=0,c_fn=0 (calibration.ts:85-92). Then gate.ts:122 tau = 0/(0+0) = NaN. utility > NaN+0.05 => false; utility < NaN-0.05 => false; falls to slow branch: regret_if_silent=p_need*0=0, cost_if_speak=(1-p_accept)*0=0, intervene = (0>0)=false. Returned decision has tau=NaN (audit records "NaN").

title: Timer recovery uses raw remainingMs in setTimeout — delays > ~24.8 days overflow and fire immediately
description: recoverTimers schedules setTimeout(cb, remainingMs) where remainingMs = end - now. Node's setTimeout stores delay in a 32-bit int; any value > 2147483647 ms (~24.8 days) is clamped to 1, firing effectively immediately. No clamp or re-scheduling logic guards this.
failure_scenario: A timer row with an end_ts more than ~24.8 days in the future (e.g. a long-horizon reminder) survives a restart. recoverTimers computes remainingMs > 2^31, setTimeout fires almost instantly, speakWithRetry announces 'Timer up' prematurely, and the row is marked fired=1 — the real deadline is lost.
fix_suggestion: Clamp the setTimeout delay to a safe max (e.g. 2^31-1) and, if remainingMs exceeds it, re-schedule in chunks (or re-run recoverTimers periodically) so long timers fire at the correct time.
reasoning: src/index.ts:41-48 schedules setTimeout(() => {...}, remainingMs).unref() where remainingMs = end.getTime() - now.getTime() (line 31), with no clamp. Node.js stores the setTimeout delay in a 32-bit signed int; any delay > 2147483647 ms (~24.8 days) is coerced to 1 ms (with a TimeoutOverflowWarning), firing the callback almost immediately. That callback speaks 'Timer up' via speakWithRetry and sets fired = 1 (line 44), permanently losing the real deadline. The technical Node behavior is certain. Reachability: timers are created only via scheduleTimer(label, minutes) from voice intent (src/pi-engine/intent.ts:216-230) or the set_timer agent tool (src/agent/tools.ts:159-162). The sole guard is a minutes <= 0 rejection (tools.ts:160) — there is NO upper bound, so an input like 40000 minutes (2.4e9 ms > 2^31) is accepted, stored as end_ts, survives a restart, and overflows in recoverTimers. So the trigger is genuinely reachable. Two caveats lower the practical severity: (1) the same unclamped overflow already exists at creation time in tools.ts:75 and intent.ts:122, so recovery is not uniquely at fault; (2) the app models timers as short minute-based countdowns and has no real 'long-horizon reminder' feature, so a >24.8-day timer requires pathological input and is unlikely in practice. The defect is real but low-impact for this system.
repro:
1. Set a timer with a huge duration, e.g. voice command 'set a timer for 40000 minutes' or set_timer tool with minutes=40000. intent.ts:221 n=40000, minutes=40000 passes the >0 check; scheduleTimer inserts a timers row with end_ts = now + 40000*60000 ms.
2. Restart the daemon before it fires. main() -> recoverTimers() (src/index.ts:123).
3. recoverTimers (index.ts:29-48): end.getTime()-now.getTime() ≈ 2.4e9 ms, which is > 2147483647. remainingMs > 0 so it takes the setTimeout branch at line 41. Node clamps the 2.4e9 ms delay to 1 ms (TimeoutOverflowWarning) and the callback runs almost immediately: speakWithRetry('Timer up: <label>.') and UPDATE timers SET fired = 1. The real ~27-day deadline is lost.
Proof lines: src/index.ts:31 (remainingMs = end.getTime() - now.getTime()) and src/index.ts:48 (}, remainingMs).unref();) with no Math.min clamp; input path has no upper bound at src/agent/tools.ts:160 / src/pi-engine/intent.ts:221.

title: Missing indexes on audit_log.ts and notifications.ts cause full-table scans on every scheduler tick
description: pruneCaches runs on every tick (default every 30s) and executes DELETE FROM audit_log WHERE ts < ? and DELETE FROM notifications WHERE ts < ?. Neither audit_log nor notifications has an index on ts (contrast events, which has events_ts_idx at line 26). audit_log is retained 90 days and notifications 30 days, so these tables grow large before pruning.
failure_scenario: After weeks of operation, audit_log holds tens of thousands of rows (tick_fired is appended on every fired tick). Every 30s the prune performs a full-table scan on audit_log and notifications to find expired rows, holding the single SQLite writer lock longer than necessary and competing with agent-loop writes. Latency and lock contention grow with table size.
fix_suggestion: Add CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS audit_log_ts_idx ON audit_log(ts) and notifications_ts_idx ON notifications(ts) so the retention DELETEs are index range scans.
reasoning: The code matches the finding exactly. In src/db.ts the `notifications` table (lines 53-58) and `audit_log` table (lines 69-76) are created with no index on their `ts` column, whereas `events` gets `events_ts_idx` at line 26. pruneCaches (src/db.ts:259-291) runs `DELETE FROM audit_log WHERE ts < ?` (line 282) and `DELETE FROM notifications WHERE ts < ?` (line 287). It is invoked at the top of runTickOnce (src/scheduler.ts:99), which runs on every scheduler tick via setInterval at config.tickIntervalSec (src/scheduler.ts:162), defaulting to 30s (src/config.ts:12). audit_log grows because tick_fired is appended on every fired tick (src/scheduler.ts:134). Without a ts index SQLite must full-scan both tables on every tick to evaluate the range predicate — including the common case where nothing has yet expired and 0 rows are deleted — while holding the single writer lock. The failure scenario is mechanically sound. I downgrade medium->low: this is a single-user on-device SQLite DB (WAL, busy_timeout=5000, "<50 MB typical"), where a full scan of even tens of thousands of small rows is single-digit ms and the DELETE holds a brief write txn regardless; the contention/battery cost is real but minor at this scale. The two-line index fix is correct and worthwhile.
repro: src/db.ts:53-58 (notifications, no ts index) and 69-76 (audit_log, no ts index) vs 26 (events_ts_idx). src/db.ts:282 `DELETE FROM audit_log WHERE ts < ?` and :287 `DELETE FROM notifications WHERE ts < ?` are full scans. Called every tick: src/scheduler.ts:99 pruneCaches(now) inside runTickOnce; :162 setInterval(safeTick, config.tickIntervalSec*1000); src/config.ts:12 default 30s. Fix: CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS audit_log_ts_idx ON audit_log(ts); CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS notifications_ts_idx ON notifications(ts);

title: Scheduler setInterval is never cleared on shutdown; ticks fire against a closed DB during the drain window
description: startScheduler creates setInterval(safeTick, ...) but never stores or clears the handle. setupGracefulShutdown (index.ts) closes the DB but does not stop the scheduler. During the up-to-5s window between db.close() and forced process.exit, the interval can fire runTickOnce, which uses prepared statements against a closed handle.
failure_scenario: On SIGTERM the DB is closed, then ~within the same second the scheduler interval fires: pruneCaches/loadHeartbeat/skill writes throw on the closed DB. safeTick catches it, but it also attempts auditAppend('scheduler_error') on the closed DB (also throws, swallowed) — noisy, and any tick-in-progress work is abandoned mid-way rather than cleanly stopped.
fix_suggestion: Return the interval handle from startScheduler (or store it), and clearInterval() it as the first step of shutdown before closing the DB.
reasoning: The defect is real and exactly as described. src/scheduler.ts:162 calls setInterval(safeTick, config.tickIntervalSec*1000) and discards the handle; startScheduler returns void so no caller can clear it. src/index.ts setupGracefulShutdown closes the DB at line 82 (db.close()) but never stops the scheduler interval; it only schedules a forced process.exit(0) at 5000ms (unref'd) at line 89-92. runTickOnce (scheduler.ts:96-147) uses module-level prepared statements (lastRunStmt/setLastRunStmt, lines 91-94) and pruneCaches (db.ts:271-288, DELETE statements) against the shared db handle. A grep confirms an isShuttingDown() guard exists (db.ts:239) and is honored in voice.ts, agent/tools.ts, and pi-engine/intent.ts — but NOT in scheduler.ts, so the scheduler has no shutdown gate. With default tickIntervalSec=30 (config.ts:12), the interval's next fire lands within the 5s forced-exit window in ~5/30 (~17%) of shutdowns since SIGTERM timing is uncorrelated with the interval phase; when it does, pruneCaches/lastRunStmt/setLastRunStmt throw on the closed handle. A tick already mid-flight (awaiting a skill/LLM consult) when db.close() runs throws on its next statement regardless of interval timing. All errors are swallowed by the try/catch at scheduler.ts:142-145 and safeTick's .catch at 156-159, and the follow-up auditAppend('scheduler_error') on the closed DB is swallowed by the inline try/catch at line 158 — matching the finding's 'noisy, swallowed' claim. Severity low is correct: the WAL is checkpointed before close (index.ts:81), only scheduler_state + audit rows are lost, errors don't propagate, and the process exits at 5s regardless.
repro: 1. Set TICK_INTERVAL_SEC low enough (or just accept ~17% odds at default 30s) and start the daemon. 2. Let a tick begin (or wait for the interval phase). 3. Send SIGTERM. index.ts shutdown() runs: setShuttingDown(true) (line 66), then db.close() (line 82), then an unref'd 5s forced-exit timer (line 89). 4. Within that 5s window the non-cleared interval (scheduler.ts:162) fires safeTick -> runTickOnce -> pruneCaches()/lastRunStmt.get()/setLastRunStmt.run() execute against the closed DatabaseSync handle and throw. 5. The throw is caught at scheduler.ts:142 which calls auditAppend('tick_error') (also against the closed DB); the rejection reaches safeTick's .catch (156-159) which attempts auditAppend('scheduler_error') inside try{}catch{} (line 158) — swallowed. Proof lines: scheduler.ts:162 (uncleared interval), scheduler.ts:99/109/141 (statements on shared db), index.ts:82 (db.close during shutdown), grep showing no isShuttingDown() call in scheduler.ts.
